name: The Syrophoenician woman
notes: Matthew and Mark recount the same encounter in the region of Tyre and Sidon, where a Gentile woman pleads with Jesus for her demon-possessed daughter and prevails through humble, persistent faith. Both share the core: the journey to the coasts/borders of Tyre and Sidon, the woman and her afflicted daughter, the saying about the children's bread and the dogs, her reply about the crumbs, Jesus' word releasing the daughter, and the healing. The two accounts give complementary vantages: Matthew names her a woman of Canaan and draws out the disciples' plea to send her away and Jesus' word that he was sent unto the lost sheep of the house of Israel, framing the scene around Israel's mission; Mark identifies her as a Greek, a Syrophenician by nation, notes the house Jesus entered, and closes by showing the daughter found laid upon the bed with the devil gone. Matthew's unique matter about the disciples and the lost sheep of Israel has no Markan parallel and reads in place. Where the wording of her plea and the closing scene differ, the accounts complement one another as two true witnesses to one event.
